_note: Original Practice questions aligned with CS Navigator's original Learn lesson for this topic.

questions.title: Tuples practice 1
choices: It raises a TypeError, because a tuple cannot be changed
It prints ('small', 'regular', 'large')
It prints ('small', 'medium', 'large') and ignores the change
It raises a syntax error, because tuples use square brackets
questions.explanation: Reading `sizes[1]` is allowed, so the line looks harmless. Assigning to it is not, and Python stops the program with a TypeError. Nothing gets ignored quietly, and nothing gets changed. If you picked the second answer, you were reading `sizes` as a list, which is the exact swap this error exists to catch.

questions.title: Tuples practice 2
choices: int tuple
tuple tuple
int int
tuple int
questions.explanation: The comma is what makes a tuple, not the parentheses. `(7)` has no comma, so the parentheses only group the number, and `a` is a plain int. `b` has the trailing comma, so it really is a one-item tuple. If you answered "tuple tuple", you trusted the parentheses, which is the reasonable guess and the one Python does not share.

questions.title: Trace Tuples
questions.prompt: What output does this code produce? Enter each line in order.
questions.explanation: You build a tuple with parentheses and commas. Reading from it looks exactly like reading from a list, so if you have seen indexing before, you already know this part. The resulting output is '(3, 5)\n3\n2'.

questions.title: Tuples good practice
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Tuples?
choices: The values will never change, like a coordinate or a fixed pair of settings: use a tuple
Skip input checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide every error and continue without checking the result.
Put all of the work in one long statement.
questions.explanation: The values will never change, like a coordinate or a fixed pair of settings: use a tuple This keeps the code easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Tuples practice 5
questions.prompt: Which version follows the lesson's safer approach?
choices:
point = (3, 5)
point = (10, 5)
# you cannot edit a tuple,
# so make a new one and
# reuse the name

point = (3, 5)
point[0] = 10
# TypeError: 'tuple' object
# does not support item
# assignment
Both versions are equally safe.
Neither version can run.
questions.explanation: This is the mistake almost everyone makes on their first tuple. The index reads fine, so it looks like it should also assign. Python lets you look, but it does not let you touch.

questions.title: Tuples practice 6
questions.prompt: Which statement best explains 'A tuple cannot be edited in place'?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.

If you see "'tuple' object does not support item assignment", you tried to write into something frozen. It usually means you reached for a list habit while holding a tuple. Either build a fresh tuple, or use a list if the values really do need to change.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: If you see "'tuple' object does not support item assignment", you tried to write into something frozen. It usually means you reached for a list habit while holding a tuple. Either build a fresh tuple, or use a list if the values really do need to change.

questions.title: Tuples practice 7
questions.prompt: Which statement best explains 'A tuple of one needs a trailing comma'?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.

`(4)` is not a tuple. The parentheses there are just grouping, the way they are in `(2 + 3) * 4`, so you get the plain number 4. To make a tuple with one item, add a comma: `(4,)`. It looks like a typo the first few times you write it. It is not.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: `(4)` is not a tuple. The parentheses there are just grouping, the way they are in `(2 + 3) * 4`, so you get the plain number 4. To make a tuple with one item, add a comma: `(4,)`. It looks like a typo the first few times you write it. It is not.

questions.title: Tuples practice 8
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Tuples?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
You need to add, remove, or edit items as the program runs: use a list
questions.explanation: You need to add, remove, or edit items as the program runs: use a list This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Tuples practice 9
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Tuples?
choices: A function needs to hand back two or three values at once: return a tuple and unpack it
Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: A function needs to hand back two or three values at once: return a tuple and unpack it This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Tuples practice 10
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Tuples?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
You want to be sure a function cannot alter what you passed in: pass a tuple
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: You want to be sure a function cannot alter what you passed in: pass a tuple This makes the result easier to understand and verify.
